A BAJATROY Tutorial
How to Opt Out of AI Data Training
A no-nonsense guide to taking back control of your digital footprint — from someone who relies on AI every single day.
10 slides · read at your own pace

Why I take this personally

AI has become one of the most powerful tools in my daily life. I use it for writing, scheduling, music creation, problem-solving, and even staying socially connected. For me, it is not just a convenience — it is a support system.

Living with a traumatic brain injury means that tasks often take longer, memory can be unreliable, and staying organized requires extra effort. AI helps bridge that gap. It allows me to return to projects, pick up where I left off, and actually finish things that might otherwise get lost.

"That is why I actively encourage people to explore and use AI. It is like having access to an endless library, with a highly capable assistant ready to help you find what you need, when you need it." — BAJATROY

Whether you are learning something new, creating music, studying, or just trying to stay on track, AI can be incredibly empowering. But with that power comes responsibility.

Why this matters right now

Every search, every chat, every tap you make is being logged, packaged, and fed into machine learning models. Most people have no idea it is happening, and even fewer know how to stop it.

72%
Feel like nearly all their online activity is being tracked
Source: Pew Research Center
81%
Say they have little to no control over their data
Source: Pew Research Center
81%
Believe the risks of tracking outweigh the benefits
Source: Pew Research Center
$10M
Average cost of a data breach in the U.S.
Source: IBM Security / Cost of a Data Breach Report
"Because of my TBI, I know I am more vulnerable to things like oversharing, confusion, or even scams. That is not a weakness — it is just a reality I have learned to work with. And it is exactly why I believe education around AI privacy and opt-out options is so important." — BAJATROY
The bottom line: Data breaches hit a record 3,322 incidents recently — a 79% surge over five years. Your data is valuable, and bad actors know it.
Source: Identity Theft Resource Center (ITRC)

The trade-off: what you are really choosing

Opting in or out is not just a settings toggle — it is a strategic decision. Here is what each path looks like in practice.

Opting in — the good
Hyper-personalization
Tailored responses, predictive suggestions, smart home integrations that actually work. Everything feels seamless because the system knows you.
Opting in — the bad
Information leakage
Proprietary code, personal conversations, and identity data can end up in public training sets. Targeted attacks on corporate data are rising.
Opting out — the good
Attack surface reduction
Complete autonomy over your data. No behavioral profiling, no targeted ads, and immunity from long-term data exploitation.
Opting out — the downside
Fragmented experience
Every session starts blank. Search results go generic. Smart features break. You trade convenience for control.
"I am not against AI training or progress. In fact, I strongly support it. But I also believe that people should have the ability to choose how their information is used. That choice should be clear, accessible, and respected." — BAJATROY

Step 1: Find your activity dashboard

Most major platforms keep a centralized portal where all your tracked activity lives. This is ground zero for taking control.

1
Open any web browser
Desktop or mobile — does not matter. Use the browser you normally use.
2
Navigate to your account's activity page
Look for a link called "My Activity," "Activity Controls," or "Privacy Dashboard" in your account settings. It is usually buried under privacy or data settings.
3
Sign in if needed
Make sure you are signed into the account you actually want to manage. Sounds obvious, but easy to miss.
Pro tip: If you cannot find it, search your account settings for the word "activity." That is the magic keyword most platforms use.

Step 2: Turn off web & app tracking

Once you are inside the activity dashboard, you will see different categories of data being collected. The big one to hit first is web and app activity.

1
Find "Web & App Activity"
This is the master switch that controls whether your searches, browsing history, and app interactions get saved.
2
Toggle it off
Flip the switch to the off position. Some platforms will warn you about losing features — that is normal. Confirm the change.
3
Delete existing history (optional but recommended)
While you are there, look for a "Delete" or "Clear" button. Wipe the old data too — turning off the tap does not clean the sink.
"Using an alias like Baja Troy is one way I protect myself. Learning how to limit data sharing is another. These are not extreme measures — they are practical steps that help me continue using AI safely while still benefiting from everything it offers." — BAJATROY

Step 3: Disable AI model training

This is the big one. Even if you turn off general tracking, many platforms still use your conversations and inputs to train their AI models. Here is how to shut that down.

1
Look for "AI Activity" or "Model Training"
Inside the same activity dashboard, find the section specifically about AI or model training. Names vary — could be "AI Apps Activity," "Chat History," or "Improve Models."
2
Toggle the switch off
Flip it to off. The platform may warn you that this prevents "improvements" to the service. That is exactly what we want.
3
Review and confirm
Some platforms ask if you want to delete existing conversation history too. Say yes. Wipe it clean.
What this stops: Your prompts, responses, and conversation patterns will no longer be saved or used to refine future AI models. Each chat starts fresh.

What actually changes after you opt out

Do not be surprised when things feel different. Here is the honest breakdown of what works better, what works worse, and what just... stops.

Feature Before (tracking on) After (opted out)
AI responses Tailored using your past conversations and habits Each session is independent — no memory of you
Search suggestions Predictive based on your history Generic results, no reflection of your past
Recommendations Local spots, routes, media based on your trends Manual input required for relevance
Data exposure Conversations may feed into training sets Your inputs are not retained or used for models
Convenience High — everything learns you Lower — you trade ease for control
Reality check: Some platforms make opting out intentionally hard to find. If you cannot locate the toggle, search their help center for "opt out of AI training" or "delete chat history."
You now have the map
Opting out is not about being paranoid — it is about being informed. You know the trade-offs. You know the steps. You know what changes. The rest is up to you.
"If you are using AI — or thinking about starting — take the time to understand your settings. Learn what you are agreeing to. Decide what you are comfortable sharing. AI should work for you, not the other way around." — BAJATROY
More privacy Less tracking Less convenience Fewer personalized features
Support the work
I create tutorials, reviews, and tools to help people use technology safely and effectively — especially those navigating disabilities like TBI. Every bit of support helps me keep going.

What I am working toward

Right now I need a few key pieces of equipment to keep creating content: four Triggerbytes and a reliable hard drive for storage and backups. These are not luxuries — they are the tools I need to keep producing tutorials like this one.

If you or someone you know would like to sponsor this work, I would be grateful for the opportunity to partner up.

"I am not against AI training or progress. In fact, I strongly support it. But I also believe that people should have the ability to choose how their information is used. That choice should be clear, accessible, and respected." — BAJATROY

Knowledge is the only real opt-in.

References & sources

All statistics and research cited in this tutorial are attributed to their original sources. This tutorial is for educational purposes only.

  1. Pew Research Center. "Public Attitudes Toward Data Privacy and Tracking." Survey data on consumer tracking perceptions, control over personal data, and risk-benefit assessments. pewresearch.org
  2. Identity Theft Resource Center (ITRC). Annual Data Breach Report. Statistics on U.S. data compromise events, breach trends, and victim notifications. idtheftcenter.org
  3. IBM Security. "Cost of a Data Breach Report." Annual industry analysis of data breach costs, root causes, and mitigation strategies. ibm.com/security
  4. Verizon / IBM Security. Industry metrics on human element involvement in infrastructure breaches (social engineering, credential reuse, shadow AI usage). verizon.com/business/dbir
Note: BAJATROY quotes and personal narrative are original content. All third-party data is cited for attribution and educational use under fair use principles.
